evidence_citations.excerpt: I think my eye for design and the way I play with color will stay with me
I think it's the way color lets me experiment without judgment, like a safe space where I can fail and still feel something
I keep thinking about all the little things that made this place feel like home, and now I have to decide where they'll go
But even that feels fragile because I still doubt whether my work is good enough, and that doubt gnaws at my sense of worth

transcript.text: I keep thinking about all the little things that made this place feel like home, and now I have to decide where they'll go. The move feels like a reset button, but also a reminder that I don't know what I'm doing. I keep worrying that I'll lose my sense of self in the process.

You're facing a lot of uncertainty with this move, and it's understandable that you're worried about losing your sense of self. It sounds like you're feeling a bit unmoored, and that's a really scary place to be. Can you tell me more about what you think will happen if you do lose your sense of self - what's the worst-case scenario in your mind?

I guess the worst thing would be to feel like I'm just a stranger in my own life, like my work and my identity are all about to dissolve. I worry that I won't even be able to recognize myself after the move.

You're really scared that this move will erase the things that make you, you - your work, your identity, all the little things that feel like home. It's like you're anticipating a complete reset, and that's terrifying because it feels like you'll lose control over who you are. Yeah, that's a lot to take in. Can you think of any parts of yourself that might stay constant, even as you're navigating all these changes?

I think my eye for design and the way I play with color will stay with me, but I'm still terrified that my self-worth will slip away because I doubt my talent. The move feels like a reset, and that anxiety about losing my identity is hard to shake.

It's really interesting that you mention your eye for design and how you play with color as things that will stay with you. Those are such unique aspects of who you are, and it's almost as if they're a part of your foundation, regardless of where you are. Yeah, it makes sense that you'd worry about your self-worth slipping away, especially since you doubt your talent, but it's also possible that these creative elements of yourself could be a constant that helps you navigate this transition. What do you think might help you hold onto these parts of yourself as you move forward?

I guess the only thing I can rely on is the design stuff, but I still feel like I'm losing my footing. The move feels like a cliff and I'm scared I might fall. Maybe I could keep a sketchbook at the new place to remind myself I'm still here.

Your design skills have been a constant source of comfort for you, and it's great that you're thinking of ways to hold onto that, like keeping a sketchbook at the new place. It's like you're trying to anchor yourself to something familiar, and that makes sense given how overwhelming this move feels. What do you think it is about designing and playing with color that makes you feel more grounded and connected to yourself?

I think it's the way color lets me experiment without judgment, like a safe space where I can fail and still feel something. But even that feels fragile because I still doubt whether my work is good enough, and that doubt gnaws at my sense of worth. I keep worrying that this next chapter will just erase that safety.

The way you use color and design as a safe space to experiment and express yourself is really beautiful, and it's clear that it brings you a sense of comfort and calm. It makes sense that you'd worry about losing that sense of safety and security in this next chapter, especially when you're already doubting your abilities. It's like you're afraid of losing the one thing that makes you feel truly like yourself. Yeah, that's a really scary feeling. I want to acknowledge how fragile this all feels for you right now, and how much your sense of self-worth is tied to your creative expression.

Thanks for saying that. Still, every time I open my sketchbook I feel this nagging doubt that my work isn’t good enough, and the move just amplifies that voice. It’s hard to imagine anything else feeling safe now.
Whatever you want to say next, I'm listening.

I don't know how to start, but the move feels like a cliff and I keep thinking I’ll fall. Every time I sketch, I doubt my work is good enough and that people will judge me. I’m scared that this change will erase the safety I find in design.

You're standing at the edge of this huge change, and it's terrifying because it feels like everything that makes you, you, is on shaky ground. Your design work, which has always been a source of comfort and safety, now feels uncertain because of the doubt that's been creeping in. It's like every time you pick up a sketchbook, you're not just creating, you're also facing this nagging voice that tells you you're not good enough. That must be really overwhelming, especially when the move itself already feels like a cliff you're staring down. Can you tell me more about what you think would help you feel more grounded in your creativity during this transition?

Maybe if I set up a small studio corner and keep a daily sketch ritual, I could feel more anchored. But I'm still worried that people will judge my work, and that doubt keeps creeping in. I guess the biggest help would be to remind myself that my worth isn't tied to one project. I just need something tangible to hold onto.

Setting up a small studio corner and keeping a daily sketch ritual could be a great way to feel more grounded in your creativity during this transition. It's like you're trying to create a sense of continuity and comfort in the midst of all this change. Yeah, it makes sense that you'd still worry about people judging your work, but it's also possible that this daily ritual could help you develop a sense of self-worth that's not tied to just one project. What do you think would be the first step in making this studio corner a reality, and how might that help you feel more connected to your creative self?
I think the first thing to do is pull out a few of my old sketches and hang them on a wall in that corner, but I keep worrying that anyone who sees them will judge my work.

You're thinking of pulling out old sketches and hanging them in your new studio corner, but the fear of judgment is holding you back. That's understandable, given how much your creative work means to you. It's like you're not just sharing your art, you're sharing a part of yourself. Yeah, it's scary to think about others seeing it, but maybe this is also an opportunity to practice embracing your unique perspective and style, imperfections and all. What if you started by just enjoying the process of selecting which sketches to hang, and worried less about what others might think?

I do want to pick out the sketches, but I keep imagining people looking at them and judging me. It feels like I'm putting my confidence on display, and I worry it will just confirm my doubts. The thought of it happening makes my whole move feel even more uncertain.

You're imagining people looking at your sketches and judging you, and that's making you feel like your confidence is on display. It's like you're worried that sharing your art will confirm your doubts, rather than showcase your talents. Yeah, that's a really vulnerable feeling. What if, instead of focusing on what others might think, you tried to enjoy the process of selecting which sketches to hang, and reminded yourself that your worth isn't defined by one project or one person's opinion?

turn_traces.user_message: I keep thinking about all the little things that made this place feel like home, and now I have to decide where they'll go. The move feels like a reset button, but also a reminder that I don't know what I'm doing. I keep worrying that I'll lose my sense of self in the process.

common_candidates.label: Support
common_candidates.what: Sympathetic / understanding statement
common_candidates.transition_fn: Sustains engagement at any stage; especially during distress / resistance

common_candidates.label: Facilitate
common_candidates.what: Brief encouragement to continue ('mm-hmm', 'go on')
common_candidates.transition_fn: Sustains conversation flow at any stage

common_candidates.label: Complex Reflection
common_candidates.what: Reflect with added inferred meaning, depth, or shift in emphasis
common_candidates.transition_fn: Develops discrepancy → drives precontemplation → contemplation

common_candidates.label: Reframe
common_candidates.what: Offer alternative interpretation that widens or shifts meaning
common_candidates.transition_fn: Widens stuck frame → drives precontemplation → contemplation

common_candidates.label: Inform (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Provide information after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Builds awareness (pre → contempl) or supports informed decision (contempl → prep)

common_candidates.label: Raise Concern (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Voice concern about a plan or pattern after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Develops discrepancy gently (pre → contempl) or surfaces risks to refine plan (prep → action)

common_candidates.label: Evoke
common_candidates.what: Invite client's own reasons for change in their words (counselor-side mirror of MISC client 'Change Talk')
common_candidates.transition_fn: Core MI move for resolving ambivalence → drives contemplation → preparation

common_candidates.label: Closed Question
common_candidates.what: Question that limits answer to yes/no/short — used sparingly
common_candidates.transition_fn: Confirms commitment or specific plan elements (prep → action)

common_candidates.label: Advise (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Suggest a course of action after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action; supports change planning

common_candidates.label: Structure
common_candidates.what: Explain what will happen next in the session or turn
common_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action by structuring the change plan

response.reasoning: I'm here as a supportive friend, and you're looking to express your emotions about this big change. Given your perfectionistic and self-critical tendencies, I think a good approach would be to use complex_reflection to help you explore your feelings. The evidence that stands out to me is your worry about losing your sense of self, which suggests that this move is tapping into some deep-seated fears. My entry point will be to acknowledge the uncertainty and overwhelm you're feeling, and see if we can't break down some of these concerns together.

evidence_used.content: self-worth
I must be perfect to be worthy
I keep worrying that I'll lose my sense of self in the process

response.reasoning: I'm here as a supportive friend, and you're expressing a lot of emotion about this upcoming move and how it's affecting your sense of self-worth and creativity. Given your intent to express emotion, I think a good strategy would be to use complex reflection to help you explore your feelings and thoughts. From what you've shared, it seems like setting up a small studio corner and keeping a daily sketch ritual could be a helpful way to feel more anchored and connected to your creative self. Your fear of being judged and the doubt that's been creeping in are also important to acknowledge, as they seem to be closely tied to your sense of worth and identity. By recognizing that your worth isn't tied to one project, you're taking a significant step towards reframing your relationship with your creativity and self-worth.

response.final_response: Setting up a small studio corner and keeping a daily sketch ritual could be a great way to feel more grounded in your creativity during this transition.
